body: Section 5: Initial Eligibility Determination A. Household Concept 1. General Household Definition Household Composition - Household composition is based primarily on food units. A food unit consists of individuals customarily purchasing food and preparing meals together for home consumption. Districts shall generally accept a household's statement as to which individuals residing with the household are members, unless for some reason the household composition is questionable. A household is composed of any of the following individuals or groups of individuals: a. An individual living alone b. An individual living with others but customarily purchasing food and preparing meals for home consumption separate and apart from others or, c. A group of individuals who live together and customarily purchase food and prepare meals together for home consumption. NOTE: Customarily purchasing and preparing means for the majority of meals in a calendar month. Majority is defined as purchasing and preparing 50% or more of three meals daily in a calendar month. NOTE: An individual away from the household for military assignment is not counted as a member of the SNAP household. Household members that are enlisted in the National Guard serving one weekend per month shall have a separate eligibility determination. NOTE: A person cannot participate as a member of more than one household at a time unless the person is a member of a reconstituted household and/or resident of a shelter for battered women and children and was a member of a household containing the person who has abused him or her. 2. Joint Custody a. Determining Household Composition: Household composition must be decided on a case-by-case basis in joint custody situations. The fundamental applicable rule is that no individual can receive SNAP benefits as a member of more than one household in any month. Some factors as parental control, primary residence, the court order, if any, and similar considerations must be evaluated. However, the duration of time, per se, which a child spends with one parent in joint situations is not necessarily the primary determining factor in whether the child is a member of that parent's household or not. b. Changes In Circumstances - So long as a child visits or resides part of the time with a parent, the child may be included in the parent's SNAP household provided that the child is not a member of another participating SNAP household. The child would remain a member of the parent's SNAP household unless: • The child is removed on a long-term basis, such as for a summer vacation of several months’ duration; or • It becomes appropriate to add the child to a different SNAP household. (See subparagraph “d” below, about district responsibility in joint custody situations.) Once in receipt of SNAP benefits as a member of one household, the child must be deleted from that household before being added to a different SNAP household. • (See also Households with Special Circumstances as affected by Simplified Reporting in Section 6 of the SNAPSB) c. Documentation - All decisions and client conversations (attestations) regarding household composition or other information relevant in joint custody situations should be thoroughly and accurately documented in the case record for Quality Control purposes. d. District Responsibility - If both parents in a joint custody situation wish to receive benefits for the same child, the issue must be resolved between the parents. District responsibility only requires insuring against duplicate participation by any individual. In addition, it is always preferable to include a joint custody child in a SNAP household than to decide if the results in the child being ineligible for SNAP benefits at all. 3. Terminated Parental Rights When a parent’s rights have been legally terminated and the parent moves into a household that includes their child[ren] who are being cared for by and under the parental control of someone else, the parent may be considered a separate SNAP household if they declare they purchase and prepare meals separately. Example 1: Grandma with legal custody (or otherwise exercising parental control) over her minor grandchildren when the children’s mother, whose parental rights have been legally terminated, returns to the household. The question is what SNAP household composition is mandated. Must they all be one household? Grandma, because she is exercising parental control over these minor children, must be in the same SNAP household as the children. Mom, because her parental rights have been legally terminated, is not required to be part of the same SNAP household as her children. Example 2: An emancipated minor (not under parental control*) returning to a household that does not include the minor’s biological parent or parents, or a child under the age of 22 returning to the household of a parent whose parental rights were terminated. In either case, the child may be a separate SNAP household if he/she declare that they purchase and prepare meals separately. * A child must be considered to be under parental control if he or she is financially or otherwise dependent on a member of the household. 4. Danks Co-op Person(s) and Essential Person(s) a. Determining Household Composition - The presence of a TA Danks Co-op Person(s) or an Essential Person(s) does not alter the method for determining SNAP household composition. That is, SNAP household composition continues to be primarily determined based on which members residing together purchase and prepare their food together. The TA Danks Co-op Person(s) or TA Essential Person(s) may, therefore, be determined as member(s) of the SNAPS household that includes other individuals with whom he or she resides, or they may be determined to be their own separate SNAP household. b. Separate Household - If the TA Danks Co-op Person(s) or TA Essential Person is a separate SNAP household they must be processed the same as any other separate SNAP household and be required to file a separate SNAP application and receive all required SNAP notices. If the Essential Person(s) is a separate SNAP household but is not a separate TA case, a separate NTA/SNAP case must be opened on WMS in order to separately issue SNAP benefits. However, if the Essential Person has a separate TA case opened on WMS for purposes of separately being issued their TA benefits, their SNAP benefits may be issued as part of that TA/SNAP case. c. Member of TA Household - If the Essential Person(s) is determined a member of the SNAP household that includes other TA members, he/she will be issued their SNAP benefits included with the other TA members, as part of the main TA household's TA/SNAP case. References: 387.1 Definitions 03 INF 10 7 CFR 273.1(a)(2) Household Concept 7 CFR 273.1(c)(1) Household Concept 7 CFR 273.3 (a) Residency 7 CFR 273.2 (f) (1) (x) Office Operations and Application Processing LDSS-4314 Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) Benefits Household Composition Desk Guide. FNS 6/12/2006 Letter- Separate Household Status for Disabled Persons 03 ADM-07 Temporary Assistance: New Shelter Allowances and Related Changes 24-ADM-11 - New Temporary Assistance Recertification Periods and Corresponding Verification Requirements for Household Composition and Residence LDSS-3666 TA/FS DOCUMENTATION/VERIFICATION DESK GUIDE ABEL Transmittal # 03-5, Information #3: Changes to Cooperative Budgeting of Shelter Allowances B. Special Household Definition 1. Ineligible For Non-Household/Separate Household Status The following individuals living with others or groups of individuals living together must be considered as customarily purchasing food and preparing meals together, even if they do not do so: a. A spouse of a member of the household, including same sex spouses. b. Children or stepchildren under 22 years of age, living with parents and their biological or adoptive child(ren) or stepchild(ren), regardless of whether they have children or a spouse, or regardless of whether the child is legally emancipated; NOTE: Please refer to GIS 12 TA/DC019 for policy regarding participation of 18-21 year old in NYSNIP/NYSCAP and residing with their parents. In certain situations these 18-21 year old can have a separate NYSNIP/NYSCAP case from their parents even though they are residing with their parents. c. Children under 18 years of age other than foster/KinGAP children under the parental control of an adult household member who is not the children's parent or stepparent. NOTE: The Kinship Guardianship Assistance Program (KinGAP) was signed into Law with Chapter 58 of the laws of 2010 and went into effect April 1, 2011. KinGAP, which is administered by OCFS, is a new program in New York State designed to provide monthly payments and other benefits to qualified relative guardians of foster children who have been discharged from foster care. Further information on KinGAP can be found in 11-OCFS-ADM-03: Kinship Guardianship Assistance Program (KinGAP). KinGAP payments should be treated the same as foster care payments for SNAP purposes. This gives families the choice of including the child as a member of the household, and counting the KinGAP payment as unearned income, or excluding the child and the KinGAP payment. The decision to include or exclude a child from a household’s SNAP case should depend on if the household would benefit financially by including or excluding the child. NOTE: The determination as to whether a minor child under 18 is under the parental control of a household member must be made on a case-by- case basis. Parental control is generally determined based on the minor's capability of providing for 50% or more of his or her own financial support. This support may be from sources such as wages from employment or receipt of his/her own TA grant. In the circumstance of an unrelated minor child residing with an adult, the child may not always be determined to be under the adult’s parental control. For example, a 17-year-old mother residing with an adult may be receiving TA in her own name, may be employed or may be residing with her spouse. In such situations, a strong case can be made she is not under the adult's parental control. On the other hand, a 13-year-old with a child, who has no income, would have difficulty establishing that she or he is not under parental control. The reason for the decision to allow or disallow separate household status must be thoroughly documented in the case record. 2. Eligible for Separate Household Status An otherwise eligible individual who is 60 years of age or older living with others (and the spouse of such individual) who is unable to purchase and prepare meals because he/she suffers from a disability considered permanent under the Social Security Act or suffers from a non-disease- related, severe, permanent disability may be a separate household. However, the income of others with whom the individual resides (excluding the income of the individual and spouse) cannot exceed 165 percent of the poverty level. For further budget information, see ABEL Manual, Section G - SNAP - Separate Household Status. NOTE: Residents of Drug Addiction or Alcohol Treatment and Rehabilitation Facilities and Group Living Facilities (as defined in this section) who apply through an authorized representative are not subject to the provisions of this Special Household Definition Section. Household composition for such residents shall be determined according to this section. References: 387.1 Definitions 97 ADM-4 Clarifications of Various Food Stamp Welfare Reform Provisions 95 ADM-15 Food Stamp Program Implications of the Mickey Leland Childhood Hunger Relief Act 91 INF-3 1990 Income Maintenance Regional Meetings Questions and Answers 96 LCM-83 Food Stamp Impact of New Federal Welfare Law 11 ADM-05 Temporary Assistance Budgeting: Treatment of Kinship Guardianship Assistance Program (KinGAP) Payments GIS 12 TA/DC019 Participation of 18-21 Year Olds in NYSNIP FNS Letter 7/15/2015 Same-Sex Marriage and SNAP Mandatory Household Status C. Non-Households Members Eligible For Non-Household/Separate Household Status - The following individuals, if otherwise eligible, may participate in SNAP as separate households: 1. Roomer Individuals who pay a household for lodging but not food may participate as a separate household. Before an individual is budgeted as a roomer, consideration should first be given to treating the individual as a non- household member who shares in incurring household expenses – i.e., as a shared living arrangement. 2. Live -In Attendant Individuals who live with a household to provide medical, housekeeping, childcare, or other similar personal services may participate as a separate household. References: 387.2 Responsibilities of Local Government 273.1 Household Concept LDSS-4314 Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) Benefits Household Composition Desk Guide. D. Ineligible Individuals Ineligible Individuals - The following individuals or groups of individuals are not eligible to participate: 1. Ineligible Student A student who is subject to but does not meet the eligibility requirements stated in this section. 2. Ineligible Alien An individual who does not meet the citizenship or eligible alien status set forth in this section. 3. Fraud/Intentional Program Violation An individual disqualified for fraud/IPV according to SNAPSB Section 7. 4. SSN Disqualified An individual disqualified for refusal to apply for or provide an SSN as set forth in this section. 5. SNAP Work Requirement Sanction An individual who was sanctioned for failure to comply with a SNAP work requirement contained in Section 385.12 of 18 NYCRR. NOTE: Please see section 13 and 17 for information on budgeting the income and resources of ineligible individuals. 6. Ineligible ABAWD An individual Ineligible to receive SNAP benefits for failure to meet ABAWD work requirements. Any household member that has used their three months of non-ABAWD compliance in a 36-month period is ineligible for SNAP benefits per 18 NYCRR 385.3. 7. Residents of institutional living arrangements (except for the following and as defined in this section): a. Residents of federally subsidized housing for the elderly, built under section 202 of the Housing Act of 1959 (P.L. 86-372) or section 236 of the National Housing Act (P.L. 73- 479); b. Individuals residing in public or approved nonprofit private drug or alcoholic treatment centers and children who live with their parent or parents in such drug or alcoholic treatment centers; c. Disabled persons who reside in approved group living facilities as defined in this section; d. Women or women with their children who are residing in temporary shelters for battered woman and children; e. Homeless individuals or families residing in temporary public or private non-profit shelters for homeless persons. NOTE: Children living with their incarcerated mothers are ineligible for SNAP since they are considered boarders. 8. Residents of a commercial boarding house A boarding house is an establishment which is licensed or viewed by the community as a commercial enterprise, offering meals and lodging for compensation with the intention of making a profit. The number of boarders residing in the boarding house shall not be used to determine if it is a commercial enterprise. Individuals living with others and paying reasonable compensation to the others for meals and lodging (boarders) except as specified in this section. Reasonable compensation is defined in the next section describing boarders. References: 387.1 Definitions 95 ADM-4 Public Assistance and Medical Assistance Benefits For Infants Residing With Their Incarcerated Mothers 12 INF-03 Temporary Assistance (TA) and Medicaid (MA) Benefits For Infants Residing With Their Incarcerated Mothers LDSS-4314 Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) Benefits Household Composition Desk Guide. E. Boarders Definition - A boarder is an individual who resides with others and pays reasonable compensation for lodging and meals. 1. Ineligible To Participate Boarders are ineligible to participate independent of the household providing the board. However, the household providing the boarder services may request that the boarder participate as a member of their household. 2. Boarder Status Boarder status shall not be extended to: a. Those individuals or groups of individuals described in this section including children or siblings residing with elderly or disabled parents or siblings. b. Boarder status cannot be extended to those living in an institutionalized setting, nor should boarders be considered to be residents of an institution. c. Individuals paying less than a reasonable monthly payment for meals. These persons will be counted as members of the household which provides the lodging and meals. 3. Determining Reasonable Compensation A reasonable monthly amount for meals shall be either of the following: a. Boarders whose board arrangement is for more than two meals a day shall pay an amount which equals or exceeds the Thrifty Food Plan for the appropriate size of the boarder household: or b. Boarders whose board arrangement is for two meals or less per day shall pay an amount which equals or exceeds two-thirds of the Thrifty Food Plan for the appropriate size of the boarder household. NOTE: Only the amount paid for meals shall be used provided that the amount paid for meals is distinguishable from the amount paid for lodging. 4. Household Eligibility The households with whom a boarder resides (including the household of the proprietor of a boarding house) may participate in the program if otherwise eligible. References: 387.1 Definitions 387.16 Special Provisions for Household Circumstances 273.1 Household Concept Elderly/Disabled Members F. Elderly/Disabled Members 1. Elderly/Disabled Members a. An elderly member is one who is 60 years of age or older. b. A disabled member is one who is: (1) Receiving Supplemental Security Income (SSI) benefits under Title XVI of the Social Security Act or disability or blindness payments under Titles I, II, X, XI, V, or XVI of the Social Security Act, (2) A veteran with a service-connected or non-service connected disability rated or paid as total (100%) by the Veteran's Administration (VA) or is considered by the VA to be in need of regular aid and attendance or permanently housebound, (3) A surviving spouse of a veteran and considered by the VA to be in need of regular aid and attendance or permanently housebound, (4) A surviving child of a veteran and considered by the VA to be permanently incapable of self-support, (5) A surviving spouse or child of a veteran and entitled to compensation for a service-connected death or pension benefit for a non-service connected death based on a VA determination and has a disability considered permanent under the Social Security Act. "Entitled" in this definition refers to those veterans' surviving spouses and children who are receiving the benefits stated above or have been approved for such payment. For disabilities that are considered permanent see SNAPSB Section 19. (6) Receiving Federal- or State-administered supplemental benefits under section 1616(a) of the Social Security Act provided that the eligibility to receive the benefits is based upon the disability or blindness criteria used under title XVI of the Social Security Act. (7) Receiving Federal- or State-administered supplemental benefits under section 212(a) of Public Law 93-66, (8) Receiving a Federal, State or Local government disability retirement pension because of a disability considered permanent under Section 221(i) of the Social Security Act. This includes individuals receiving payments under the Federal Employment Compensation Act (FECA). Individuals receiving FECA payments are considered permanently disabled under section 221(i) of the Social Security Act if the payments are made to a person in lieu of Civil Service Retirement (CSR) benefits. Example: NYS Disability Retirement benefits. Persons receiving FECA payments pending a determination of eligibility for CSR may not be considered disabled under this provision. Only those who can document that they have elected to receive FECA payments in lieu of CSR benefits satisfy the requirements of this provision. NOTE: Employees injured on the job who receive temporary FECA payments while they recover are not considered permanently disabled under this provision. (9) Receiving an annuity under: (a) Section 2(a)(1)(v) of the Railroad Retirement Act of 1974 and is determined to be disabled based upon the criteria used under Title XVI of the Social Security Act; or (b) Section 2(a)(1)(iv) of the Railroad Retirement Act of 1974 and is determined to qualify for Medicare by the Railroad Retirement Board; or (10) Receiving authorization of Medical Assistance (MA) based upon disability or blindness. In New York State, such medical assistance recipients are those who have been certified by Medical Assistance as blind, disabled or "SSI-related", pursuant to Title XVI. The following is a link to what disabilities are classified as permanent under the Social Security Act: http://www.socialsecurity.gov/disability/professionals/bluebook/Adul tListings.htm (11) A recipient of interim assistance benefits pending the receipt of Supplemental Security Income (SSI), provided that the eligibility to receive these benefits is based upon disability criteria which are at least as stringent as those used under Title XVI of the Social Security Act (SSA). For purposes of this part of the SNAP definition of disabled, interim assistance means any TA given to individuals applying for SSI. In New York State, interim assistance is issued to eligible individuals pending receipt of SSI. However, there is no requirement that a determination of disability be done as a condition of receiving the interim assistance. Since these individuals can be referred to SSI without a thorough disability determination or have applied on their own without a district review of their disability, they do not meet the definition of disabled while receiving interim assistance payments. However, in certain circumstances a determination of disability using criteria as strict as required by the SSA is done for interim assistance recipients before the SSI determination is completed. In such situations, the person is considered disabled while receiving interim assistance. It is anticipated that one group this provision may apply to would be individuals in receipt of the emergency shelter allowance for persons with AIDS or HIV-related illness. NOTE: The definition of elderly or disabled applies when determining eligibility for uncapped excess shelter and medical deductions and the exemption from gross income test. When households/individuals receiving SNAP while pending SSI are determined eligible for SSI, they must be re-budgeted as a household with an elderly/disabled member retroactive to the date of eligibility for SSI, or SNAP application date, whichever is later (see this section and SNAPSB Section 12. Benefits will be restored according to SNAPSB Section 11. 2. WMS/MyWorkspace Instruction Individuals described in paragraph 10 above are identified by WMS Individual Categorical Code "11- Blind" or "12- Disabled" on Screen 3 of the WMS Medical Assistance Case Record. An 'X' should be entered in the Aged-Disabled Indicator on the SNAPABEL Budget if the Individual Categorical Code is 12 or 13. An “A” should be entered in the Aged- Disabled Indicator in WMS/MyWorkspace if all SNAP household members are aged/disabled (Upstate districts only). References: 387.1 Definitions 273.2 (f) (1) (viii) Office Operations and Application Processing 94 ADM-15 Food Stamps: Expansion of Definition of Food Stamp Eligibles Who Reside in Certain Group Living Arrangements 91 ADM-15 Food Stamps: Definition of Disabled 99 LCM-1 Food Stamp Program: Clarification of Definition of Disability P.L. 93-66 SUPPLEMENTAL SECURITY INCOME BENEFITS FOR ESSENTIAL PERSONS SSA 221(i) DISABILITY DETERMINATIONS SSA Title XVI SUPPLEMENTAL SECURITY INCOME FOR THE AGED, BLIND, AND DISABLED G. Household Types 1. Temporary Assistance (TA) Household A household in which all members are applying for or receiving Family Assistance (FA) or Safety Net Assistance (SNA). 2. Mixed Household A household in which all members apply for SNAP benefits as a single unit and some of the members also apply for TA or are current TA recipients. Example: A five-person household applies for SNAP benefits. Three members of the SNAP household receive a TA grant. One member of the SNAP household who receives SSI and one member of the SNAP household who is a legally assigned foster child are not included in the TA grant. This household is a mixed household for SNAP purposes. 3. Non-Temporary Assistance (NTA) Household A household in which no one is a TA applicant or recipient. 4. "Pure" SSI Household A household in which all members are SSI applicants or recipients. References: 273.23 (d) Simplified Application and Standardized Benefit Projects H. Head of Household 1. Definition Head of Household is the member of the applicant household designated by the household to represent them in matters pertaining to its eligibility for and receipt of SNAP benefits. The head of household classification is not one required under SNAP Regulations and must not be used to impose special requirements on the household, such as requiring that the head of household, rather than another responsible member of the household, receive benefits in his/her name or apply and be interviewed for benefits. 2. Designation i. By Applicant - At time of application, the applicant should designate a household member in whose name benefits will be issued. The applicant may also designate a head of household whenever there is a change in household composition. ii. By District - If the applicant is unable to designate a member of the applicant household, or if there is no adult member, the eligibility worker shall designate the member in whose name benefits will be issued. References: 387.1 Definitions 273.1 (d) Household Concept I. Non-Financial Eligibility Standards 1. Categorical Eligibility 1. Categorically Eligible Households - Categorically Eligible households are not subject to the Gross Income Test, the Net Income Test, or the SNAP resource test. Categorically eligible households are presumed, without further investigation or verification, to meet the resource limits as a result of all members of the household being eligible for Family Assistance, non- emergency Safety Net Assistance and/or SSI. All other requirements of SNAP eligibility apply to categorically eligible households. The following households are considered categorically eligible for SNAP benefits. 1) All NTA/SNAP households that have income at or below 130% of the Federal Poverty Level (FPL) and have received a TANF-MOE funded service except (a): a. SNAP households containing an individual who has been disqualified for an Intentional Program Violation (IPV); 2) Households that have earned income, and a total gross income (both earned and unearned) at or below 150% of the federal poverty limit; and, 3) households that have out-of-pocket dependent care expenses and a gross income at or below 200% of the federal poverty limit; 4) households that have either an elderly or disabled member (or members) and a gross income at or below 200% of the federal poverty limit; Note: SNAP households containing a member who is age 60 or older, or disabled may be eligible with income above 200% of the Federal Poverty Level, but cannot be considered categorically eligible. If income is above 200% of the Federal Poverty Level, the household must pass the Net Income Test and the Resource Test. 5) Households in which all members receive or are authorized to receive TA and/or SSI benefits shall be eligible for SNAP because of their status as TA and/or SSI recipients. 6) Recipients who meet the categorical eligibility criteria because they are authorized to receive TA or SSI benefits shall include: i. Individuals determined eligible for TA and/or SSI benefits who have not yet received payments from these programs; ii. Individuals whose TA or SSI benefits are suspended or being recouped; NOTE: If an SSI recipient has his/her SSI benefits suspended, the SSI income would continue to be budgeted for SNAP only if the suspension is due to a recovery of past SSI benefits fraudulently received. (See SNAPSB Section 13) However, a household in which a member is suspended from SSI for non-compliance with Drug Addiction and/or Alcoholism treatment requirements is not categorically eligible to participate in the SNAP. The household cannot be considered categorically eligible as SSI recipients until that individual's suspension ends and the individual starts receiving SSI benefits. Because these individuals will not receive any retroactive SSI benefits for the period of suspension, they would not be "authorized to receive" SSI benefits as required for categorical eligibility. In order to regain SNAP certification, the household would have to be determined eligible under normal requirements. The SNAP eligibility determination would be calculated without including an SSI benefit as income for the suspended individual until the suspension period ends and benefits are resumed. 7) Individuals determined eligible to receive zero TA or SSI benefits; Example: TA or SSI recipients whose benefits are being recouped, or TA recipients whose benefits are less than $10 and therefore not payable. 8) Individuals initially denied SSI. If a person is denied SSI, then wins an appeal later, the SNAP categorical eligibility would be effective from the effective date of his/her SSI application (or of a SNAP application which is filed later than the SSI application) as established by the appeal. 2. Determining Household Composition All SNAP household composition provisions as defined in this section must be properly applied when establishing whether a group of people is categorically eligible. a. Compliance With Other Eligibility Requirements - Categorically eligible households must comply with all SNAP eligibility requirements. b. Application Processing - If a SNAP household has either jointly or separately filed an application for TA (FA and Federally Funded SNA) and/or SSI benefits, and NTA/SNAP benefits the application for SNAP must be processed according to the following standards: c. The determination must be made within 30 days of the SNAP application filing date. d. The SNAP eligibility determination shall not be delayed beyond the 30th day from the date the SNAP application was filed, awaiting the determination of eligibility for TA and/or SSI benefits. e. When households/individuals receiving SNAP while pending SSI are determined eligible for SSI, they must be re-budgeted as a categorically eligible elderly/disabled household retroactive to the date of eligibility for SSI or SNAP application date, whichever is later. See SNAPSB Section 11. f. In re-budgeting the SNAP case for the months covered by the retro SSI payment the monthly SSI amount cannot be budgeted as income because when the benefits were issued, the SSI had not yet been received. g. In no event, however, can SNAP benefits be provided due to categorical eligibility prior to the date of an original SNAP application filed. 3. Verification a. Households that are categorically eligible due to household members being in receipt of either TA or SSI benefits will have the following factors accepted for SNAP eligibility without further verification: (a) Resources (b) Social security numbers of the household members (c) Residency, and (d) Sponsored alien information b. All other eligibility and benefit determination factors must be verified. Questionable circumstances must also be verified. 4. Eligibility and Benefit Determination a. Pure TA/SSI households are exempt from the SNAP resource, gross income and net income limits, but the standard benefit amount calculation must be followed as for all other SNAP households. One and two person households that are categorically eligible are always entitled to at least the minimum SNAP benefit for a household of 1 or 2 persons. Categorically eligible households with three or more members will be entitled to benefits of at least $2if the Thrifty Food Plan, reduced by 30% of their net income, is at least $1. Pure TA/SSI households with three or more members will be automatically entitled to a benefit determination, but not necessarily entitled to receive SNAP benefits because of the way benefit levels are calculated. b. A household categorically eligible for zero benefits must be denied or closed rather than suspended. In such cases, the household is categorically eligible for zero benefits. 5. Households Which Lose Categorical Eligibility Households which lose SNAP categorical SNAP eligibility must be reassessed for continued SNAP eligibility. Categorically Ineligible Households - The following households are not categorically eligible: a. Households containing a member who has been disqualified from the SNAP due to an intentional program violation or other sanction. b. Households containing an aged and/or disabled member with gross income is above 200% of the Federal Poverty Level 6. WMS/MyWorkspace Instructions 1. Categorical Indicator - The CE entry needs to be changed only when SNAP household circumstances change. 1) Categorically Eligible (a) If all persons in the SNAP household are receiving TA or SSI, the household is categorically eligible for SNAP and must be coded "Y." If a "Y" is entered in the CE field, ABEL will not apply the 130% Gross Income Test nor the 100% Net Income Test. (b) NTA-SNAP households that are categorically eligible for SNAP must be coded “N”. If an “N” is entered in the CE field only the 130% Gross Income Test is applied. (c) NTA-SNAP households with earned income that are categorically eligible for SNAP benefits must be coded “N”. If an “N” is entered in the CE field only the 150% Gross Income Test is applied. (d) NTA-SNAP households with a sanctioned or disqualified individual must be coded “S” and pass the 130% Gross Income Test, 100% Net Income Test and the Resource Test. (e) SNAP households with an aged/disabled member(s) must be coded “N” and pass the 200% Gross Income Test. If the household fails the 200% Gross Income Test, then the household is not categorically eligible and is subject to the 100% Net Income Test and Resource Test. The CE indicator will automatically change to “A”. (f) SNAP households that incur dependent care expenses and pass the 200% Gross Income Test will have a CE code of “N”, unless the household members are all receiving TA and/or SSI, then the CE indicator will be “Y”. 2) Categorically Ineligible - TA households containing a sanctioned TA member who is also SNAP sanctioned are not categorically eligible and must be coded “S”. 3) Categorical Eligibility For Surplus TA Budgets Or TA Budgets With A Sanctioned Individual - ABEL requires the worker to re-evaluate SNAP categorical eligibility for TA/SNAP cases in which: (a) A person has been sanctioned from the TA case or; (b) There is a TA budget surplus. ABEL does not require the worker to re-evaluate SNAP categorical eligibility for any other TA/SNAP cases. Residency An applicant must live within the geographic area served by the district in which they file an application for SNAP benefits. To determine residency, the district must: • Determine whether or not the applicant physically resides in the district. • Not impose a time limited residency requirement. • Not interpret residency to mean the intent to permanently reside in the State or district. However, persons in the district solely for vacation purposes must not be considered residents. 1. Homeless Individual Or Family An individual or family shall be considered homeless when the individual or family lacks a fixed and regular nighttime residence or has a primary nighttime residence that is: a. A supervised shelter, such as a hotel or motel or congregate shelter, which is designed to provide temporary accommodations; or b. An institution that provides temporary residence for individuals who need to be institutionalized; or c. A place not designed for, or ordinarily used as, a regular sleeping accommodation for human beings (a hallway, a bus station, a lobby or similar places). d. A temporary accommodation in the residence of another individual or family (couch surfing). For homeless households, in addition to the above, the districts must: a. Not require an otherwise eligible household to reside in a permanent dwelling or have a fixed mailing address as a condition of eligibility. Residence can be a car, bus, etc. b. Not deny participation in the program to a homeless household when residency cannot be verified. c. Provide assistance in obtaining their benefits to households who do not reside in a permanent dwelling or have a fixed mailing address. An issuance system must be developed that assures that all certified households have access to their benefits. NOTE: A household cannot receive benefits in more than one district in any month unless an individual is a resident of a shelter for victims of domestic violence and was a member of a household containing the person who had abused him or her. Residents of shelters for victims of domestic violence shall be handled in accordance with SNAPSB Section 5. NOTE: Residents of programs for victims of domestic violence who receive TA from a district other than the district of residence also must have their SNAP case processed by the district determined to be fiscally responsible for TA provided the where from district is able to issue benefits within required timeframes. 2. Responsibilities Of Former District Of Residence a. The former district is required to inform the recipient of the need to apply for SNAP benefits in the new district if such recipient wishes to have his or her SNAP benefits continued after the effective closing date of the SNAP case from the former district. b. The former district must respond to any oral or written inquiries from the new district about the status of the SNAP case, the effective closing date of the SNAP case in the former district, or about whether SNAP benefits have been issued or received by the households. c. The former district must provide copies of documentation from the recipient's case record as requested either orally or in writing by the new district. The former district must comply with such requests as soon as possible, but in no event later than 10 days after receiving the request from the new district. d. When a recipient of TA and SNAP moves on their own to another district and continues to be eligible, the "from" district continues to provide TA and SNAP benefits during the month of the move and the month following the month of the move. Households are encouraged to apply and establish eligibility in the new "move to" district of residence during the two-month transitional period to avoid interruption of benefits. Temporary Assistance households which are continued on TA by the "move from" district, as required under district of fiscal responsibility for reasons of the Medical Facility, Placement or Temporary Absence rules, as specified in 97 INF-6 District of Fiscal Responsibility, will not be required under this policy to be continued on SNAP by the "move from" district. e. NTA/SNAP only households that move from one district to another must be provided adequate notice of closing. As a best practice, the “move from” should provide benefits for the month of the move and the month following the month of the move to prevent a lapse in benefits to the household while the household applies for benefits in the “move to”. f. If the household is residing in a domestic violence shelter and the alleged abuse occurred in the where from district, the where from district is responsible to issue SNAP benefits the duration of the stay in the shelter provided the household was receiving TA in the where from district or applying for TA at the time of the alleged abuse. 3. Responsibilities Of The New District Of Residence a. At the time an individual contacts the new district and indicates that he/she wishes to apply for SNAP benefits in that district, the new district must provide the individual with an application and must schedule a face-to-face or telephone interview as explained in SNAPSB Section 4. The new district must not refuse to give out, accept or process the application on the grounds that the recipient is, or should be, receiving SNAP benefits from the former district. b. If the recipient is unable to produce a closing notice from the former district or claims that he/she is not actually receiving appropriate SNAP benefits from the former district, it is the responsibility of the new district to contact the former district to ascertain the current status of the recipient. NOTE: Expedited application processing service should not be delayed if the district has failed to obtain verification of the case closing. If the recipient is, or claims to be, unable to provide necessary documentation which is likely to be in the possession of the former district (e.g., birth certificates, etc.), it is the responsibility of the new district to obtain documentation needed to establish the recipient's continued eligibility from the case records maintained by the former district. Documentation which is specifically relevant to the recipient's residence in the new district, such as rent costs, current household composition and income, if changed, must be obtained from the recipient or through regular documentation/verification procedures c. When the recipient applies for SNAP benefits in the new district before benefits from the former district cease, the new district must accept and process the SNAP application within normal SNAP processing requirements and time frames. However, every attempt must be made to process the application quickly and to assure that SNAP benefits to an otherwise eligible recipient are not delayed or interrupted solely because of that recipient's move into a new district. If the new district determines that the recipient is eligible before SNAP benefits from the former district have ceased, the new district must open the case effective with the date that benefits from the former district are scheduled to end. d. The initial SNAP benefits are not prorated if an application is made in a new district on or before the first day of the month following the expiration of benefits in the former county of origin. However, if application is made in the new district after the first day of the month following the expiration of SNAP benefits in the county of origin, the SNAP benefits for the first month in the new district must be prorated from the date of the application filed in the new district. SNAP eligibility may not be established until all documentation is received. However, an initial month's benefit shall not be prorated due to a delay in receiving documentation which is not the fault of the applicant. e. If the resident of a domestic violence shelter was receiving TA in another district or was applying for TA in another district at the time of the alleged abuse, and the resident qualifies for expedited processing, the expedited timeframes must still be met. If the where from district is unable to process the case within the required expedited timeframes for any reason, the where found district will provide a CBIC if necessary and issue the expedited benefits within the required expedited timeframe. The where from district will make the future ongoing payments. The two districts do not have to reconcile the benefits as they are federal funds.
