Citizenship and Non-Citizen (alien) Status 1. Household members meeting citizenship or non-citizen (alien) status requirements. No person is eligible to participate in the Program unless that person is: a. A U.S. citizen; b. A U.S. non-citizen national c. An individual who is: i) An American Indian born in Canada who possesses at least 50 per centum of blood of the American Indian race to whom the provisions of section 289 of the Immigration and Nationality Act (INA) (8 U.S.C. 1359) apply; or ii) A member of an Indian tribe as defined in section 4(e) of the Indian Self-Determination and Education Assistance Act (25 U.S.C. 450b(e)) which is recognized as eligible for the special programs and services provided by the U.S. to Indians because of their status as Indians; d. An individual who is: i) Lawfully residing in the U.S. and was a member of a Hmong or Highland Laotian tribe at the time that the tribe rendered assistance to U.S. personnel by taking part in a military or rescue operation during the Vietnam era beginning August 5, 1964, and ending May 7, 1975; ii) The spouse, or surviving spouse of such Hmong or Highland Laotian who is deceased, or iii) An unmarried dependent child of such Hmong or Highland Laotian who is under the age of 18 or if a full-time student under the age of 22; an unmarried child under the age of 18 or if a full time student under the age of 22 of such a deceased Hmong or Highland Laotian provided the child was dependent upon him or her at the time of his or her death; or an unmarried disabled child age 18 or older if the child was disabled and dependent on the person prior to the child's 18th birthday. For purposes of this section, child means the legally adopted or biological child of the person described in the paragraph above of this section, or e. An individual who is: i) A non-citizen who has been subjected to a severe form of trafficking in persons and who is certified by the Department of Health and Human Services, to the same extent as a non-citizen who is admitted to the United States as a refugee under Section 207 of the INA; or ii) A non-citizen who has been subjected to a severe form of trafficking in persons and who is under the age of 18, to the same extent as a non-citizen who is admitted to the United States as a refugee under Section 207 of the INA; iii) The spouse, child, parent or unmarried minor sibling of a victim of a severe form of trafficking in persons under 21 years of age, and who has received a derivative T visa, to the same extent as a non- citizen who is admitted to the United States as a refugee under Section 207 of the INA; or iv) The spouse or child of a victim of a severe form of trafficking in persons 21 years of age or older, and who has received a derivative T visa, to the same extent as a non-citizen who is admitted to the United States as a refugee under Section 207 of the INA; or f. An individual who is both a qualified non-citizen as defined in this section and an eligible non-citizen as defined in this section. 1) A qualified non-citizen is: a) A non-citizen who is lawfully admitted for permanent residence under the INA; b) A non-citizen who is granted asylum under section 208 of the INA; c) A refugee who is admitted to the United States under section 207 of the INA; d) A non-citizen who is paroled into the U.S. under section 212(d)(5) of the INA for a period of at least 1 year; e) A non-citizen whose deportation is being withheld under section 243(h) of the INA as in effect prior to April 1, 1997, or whose removal is withheld under section 241(b)(3) of the INA; f) A non-citizen who is granted conditional entry pursuant to section 203(a)(7) of the INA as in effect prior to April 1, 1980; g) A non-citizen who has been battered or subjected to extreme cruelty in the U.S. by a spouse or a parent or by a member of the spouse or parent's family residing in the same household as the non-citizen at the time of the abuse, a non-citizen whose child has been battered or subjected to battery or cruelty, or a non-citizen child whose parent has been battered;[2] or h) A non-citizen who is a Cuban or Haitian entrant, as defined in section 501(e) of the Refugee Education Assistance Act of 1980. 2) A qualified non-citizen, as defined in this section, is eligible to receive SNAP benefits and is not subject to the requirement to be in qualified status for 5 years as set forth in this section, if such individual meets at least one of the criteria of this section: a) A non-citizen age 18 or older lawfully admitted for permanent residence under the INA who has 40 qualifying quarters as determined under Title II of the SSA, including qualifying quarters of work not covered by Title II of the SSA, based on the sum of: quarters the non-citizen worked; quarters credited from the work of a parent of the non-citizen before the non-citizen became 18 (including quarters worked before the non-citizen was born or adopted); and quarters credited from the work of a spouse of the non-citizen during their marriage if they are still married or the spouse is deceased. (1) A spouse may not get credit for quarters of a spouse when the couple divorces prior to a determination of SNAP eligibility. However, if the State agency determines eligibility of an non-citizen based on the quarters of coverage of the spouse, and then the couple divorces, the non-citizen eligibility continues until the next recertification. At that time, the State agency must determine the non-citizen’s eligibility without crediting the non-citizen with the former spouse's quarters of coverage. (2) After December 31, 1996, a quarter in which the non-citizen received any Federal means-tested public benefit, as defined by the agency providing the benefit, or received SNAP benefits is not creditable toward the 40-quarter total. Likewise, a parent's or spouse's quarter is not creditable if the parent or spouse received any Federal means-tested public benefit or received SNAP benefits in that quarter. The State agency must evaluate quarters of coverage and receipt of Federal means-tested public benefits on a calendar year basis. The State agency must first determine the number of quarters creditable in a calendar year, then identify those quarters in which the non-citizen (or the parent(s) or spouse of the non-citizen) received Federal means-tested public benefits and then remove those quarters from the number of quarters of coverage earned or credited to the non-citizen in that calendar year. However, if the non-citizen earns the 40th quarter of coverage prior to applying for SNAP benefits or any other Federal means- tested public benefit in that same quarter, the State agency must allow that quarter toward the 40 qualifying quarters total; b) A non-citizen admitted as a refugee under section 207 of the INA; c) A non-citizen granted asylum under section 208 of the INA; d) A non-citizen whose deportation is withheld under section 243(h) of the INA as in effect prior to April 1, 1997, or whose removal is withheld under section 241(b)(3) or the INA; e) A non-citizen granted status as a Cuban or Haitian entrant (as defined in section 501(e) of the Refugee Education Assistance Act of 1980); f) An Amerasian admitted pursuant to section 584 of Public Law 100-202, as amended by Public Law 100-461; g) A non-citizen with one of the following military connections: (1) A veteran who was honorably discharged for reasons other than non-citizen status, who fulfills the minimum active-duty service requirements of 38 U.S.C. 5303A(d), including an individual who died in active military, naval or air service. The definition of veteran includes an individual who served before July 1, 1946, in the organized military forces of the Government of the Commonwealth of the Philippines while such forces were in the service of the Armed Forces of the U.S. or in the Philippine Scouts, as described in 38 U.S.C. 107; (2) An individual on active duty in the Armed Forces of the U.S. (other than for training); or (3) The spouse and unmarried dependent children of a person described in this section, including the spouse of a deceased veteran, provided the marriage fulfilled the requirements of 38 U.S.C. 1304, and the spouse has not remarried. An unmarried dependent child for purposes of this paragraph is: a child who is under the age of 18 or, if a full-time student, under the age of 22; such unmarried dependent child of a deceased veteran provided such child was dependent upon the veteran at the time of the veteran's death; or an unmarried disabled child age 18 or older if the child was disabled and dependent on the veteran prior to the child's 18th birthday. For purposes of this paragraph child means the legally adopted or biological child of the person described in this section. h) An individual who is receiving benefits or assistance for blindness or disability. The disability assistance or benefits must be predicated on a disability determination using criteria at least as stringent as the criteria used under title XVI of the Social Security Act. This includes qualified immigrants who receive Supplemental Security Income (SSI) or Social Security Disability (SDSD), or Medicaid if a disability determination has been made (WMS Individual Categorical Code 11 or 12.) i) An individual who on August 22, 1996, was lawfully residing in the U.S., and was born on or before August 22, 1931; or j) (An individual who is under 18 years of age. 3) The following qualified non-citizens, as defined in this section, must be in a qualified status for 5 years before being eligible to receive SNAP benefits. The 5 years in qualified status may be either consecutive or nonconsecutive. Temporary absences of less than 6 months from the United States with no intention of abandoning U.S. residency do not terminate or interrupt the individual's period of U.S. residency. If the resident is absent for more than 6 months, the agency shall presume that U.S. residency was interrupted unless the non-citizen presents evidence of his or her intent to resume U.S. residency. In determining whether an non-citizen with an interrupted period of U.S. residency has resided in the United States for 5 years, the agency shall consider all months of residency in the United States, including any months of residency before the interruption: a) A non-citizen age 18 or older lawfully admitted for permanent residence under the INA. b) A non-citizen who is paroled into the U.S. under section 212(d)(5) of the INA for a period of at least 1 year; c) A non-citizen who has been battered or subjected to extreme cruelty in the U.S. by a spouse or a parent or by a member of the spouse or parent's family residing in the same household as the alien at the time of the abuse, an alien whose child has been battered or subjected to battery or cruelty, or an alien child whose parent has been battered; d) A non-citizen who is granted conditional entry pursuant to section 203(a)(7) of the INA as in effect prior to April 1, 1980. 4) Each category of eligible alien status stands alone for purposes of determining eligibility. Subsequent adjustment to a more limited status does not override eligibility based on an earlier less rigorous status. Likewise, if eligibility expires under one eligible status, the State agency must determine if eligibility exists under another status. 5) For purposes of determining eligible non-citizen status in accordance with this section “lawfully residing in the U.S.” means that the non-citizen is lawfully present as defined at 8 CFR 103.12(a). 2. Eligible non-citizens [State Regulation 387.9(a)(2)] To be eligible to participate in the SNAP program, a person must be a citizen of the United States, a noncitizen national of the United States, or be a qualified eligible non-citizens. Only qualified non-citizens are eligible for federally funded benefits. A qualified non-citizen is an individual who meets the specific definition of qualified alien (non-citizen) found at 7 CFR 273.4(a)(6)(i), which includes lawful permanent residents, asylees, refugees, parolees, individuals granted withholding of deportation or removal, conditional entrants, Cuban or Haitian entrants, battered non-citizens, and non-citizen victims of a severe form of trafficking. See also PRWORA, 8 U.S.C. § 1641. The term qualified non-citizen is not itself an immigration status but includes a collection of immigration statuses. It is a term used for Federal public benefits purposes. Because of their immigration status, the federal government grants certain qualified non-citizens special access to all federal benefit programs including SNAP. OTDA identified these non-citizens as “specially qualified non- citizens”. Specially qualified non-citizens are not subject to the five year ban on federal benefits. Specially qualified non-citizens are as follows: a. Is in a qualified status and in receipt of certain disability benefits as outlined 7 USC 2012(r) b. A non-citizen admitted to the United States as a refugee under section 207 of the Immigration and Nationality Act c. A non-citizen granted asylum under section 208 of the Immigration and Nationality Act d. A non-citizen whose deportation was withheld under section 243(h) of the Immigration and Nationality Act as in effect prior to April, 1997 or whose removal was withheld under section 241(b)(3) of the Immigration and Nationality Act e. An Amerasian immigrant admitted into the United States as described in Section 402(a)(2)(A)(i)(V) of PRWORA f. A Cuban Haitian entrant (as defined in Section 501(e) of the Refugee Education Assistance Act of 1980) paroled into the United States g. The non-citizen is an Iraqi or Afghan national granted special immigration status under section 101(a)(27) of the Immigration Nationality Act Note: see the LDSS-4579 Non-Citizen Eligibility Desk Aid for more information regarding Afghan evacuees and humanitarian parolees. h. The non-citizen is a victim of severe form of trafficking in persons as defined by the Trafficking Victims Protection Act of 2000. There are two kinds of qualified victims: 1. Adults, who are certified by the Office of Refugee Resettlement (ORR) in the Department of Health and Human Services; and 2. Children under 18 years of age, who do not have to be certified but who are issued a letter by ORR stating that the child is eligible for benefits as a victim of a severe form of trafficking in persons. Samples of the ORR adult certification letter and ORR child eligibility letter are found in 03 ADM-1. These letters are for benefit eligibility purposes only. Certification and eligibility letters issued before November 6, 2001 had 8-month expiration dates. ORR recertified these victims with a recertification letter or new eligibility letter; these letters have a lower case “r” beside the HHS tracking number. Recertification letters do not have expiration dates. i. Military personnel who are legal permanent residents (also spouses and unmarried dependent children) who are on active full-time duty in a branch of the United States armed forces or Coast Guard are eligible from the date qualified status was granted j. U.S. veterans who are legal permanent residents (also spouses and unmarried dependent children) who fulfilled the 2-year minimum active duty requirement and received an “Honorable” discharge are eligible from the date qualified status was granted k. Hmong or Highland Laotian tribe members who are members of a tribe who rendered assistance to the U.S. personal by taking part in a military or rescue operation during the Vietnam era between August 5, 1964 and May 7, 1975, and who are legally residing in the U.S. The individual must be born prior to 05/08/75 and state they are a member of a Hmong or Highland Laotian tribe who rendered assistance to the U.S during the Vietnam era. Such tribe members, their spouses, their un- remarried surviving spouses and/or their unmarried children are eligible from the date qualified status was granted. l. Is age 18 or older lawfully and lawfully admitted to the United States for permanent residence under the immigration and Nationality Act; and has earned 40 qualifying quarters of coverage as defined under Title II of the Social Security Act or can be credited with such qualifying quarters. 3. Ineligible Non-Citizen (State Regulation 387.1) An ineligible non-citizen is one who is not a citizen of the United States and does not meet the eligible non-citizen criteria. A non-citizen who is a qualified non-citizen, as set forth below, is not eligible to participate in the SNAP unless otherwise provided for below. a. A qualified non-citizen is: 1. A non-citizen who is lawfully admitted for permanent residence under the INA; 2. A non-citizen who is granted asylum under section 208 of the INA; 3. A refugee who is admitted to the United States under section 207 of the INA; 4. A non-citizen who is paroled into the U.S. under section 212(d)(5) of the INA for a period of at least 1 year; 5. A non-citizen whose deportation is being withheld under section 243(h) of the INA as in effect prior to April 1, 1997, or whose removal is withheld under section 241(b)(3) of the INA; 6. A non-citizen who is granted conditional entry pursuant to section 203(a)(7) of the INA as in effect prior to April 1, 1980; 7. A non-citizen who has been battered or subjected to extreme cruelty in the U.S. by a spouse or a parent or by a member of the spouse or parent's family residing in the same household as the non-citizen at the time of the abuse, a non-citizen whose child has been battered or subjected to battery or cruelty, or a non-citizen child whose parent has been battered; or 8. A non-citizen who is a Cuban or Haitian entrant, as defined in section 501(e) of the Refugee Education Assistance Act of 1980. b. A qualified non-citizen, as defined in this section, is eligible to receive SNAP benefits and is not subject to the requirement to be in qualified status for 5 years, if such individual meets at least one of the criteria listed below: 1. A non-citizen age 18 or older lawfully admitted for permanent residence under the INA who has 40 qualifying quarters (see additional information in Section 3 below) as determined under Title II of the SSA, including qualifying quarters of work not covered by Title II of the SSA, based on the sum of: quarters the non-citizen worked; quarters credited from the work of a parent of the non-citizen before the non-citizen became 18 (including quarters worked before the non- citizen was born or adopted); and quarters credited from the work of a spouse of the non-citizen during their marriage if they are still married or the spouse is deceased. (a) A spouse may not get credit for quarters of a spouse when the couple divorces prior to a determination of SNAP eligibility. However, if the State agency determines eligibility of a non-citizen based on the quarters of coverage of the spouse, and then the couple divorces, the non-citizen’s eligibility continues until the next recertification. At that time, the State agency must determine the non-citizen's eligibility without crediting the non-citizen with the former spouse's quarters of coverage. (b) After December 31, 1996, a quarter in which the non-citizen received any Federal means-tested public benefit, as defined by the agency providing the benefit, or received SNAP benefits is not creditable toward the 40-quarter total. Likewise, a parent's or spouse's quarter is not creditable if the parent or spouse received any Federal means-tested public benefit or received SNAP benefits in that quarter. The State agency must evaluate quarters of coverage and receipt of Federal means-tested public benefits on a calendar year basis. The State agency must first determine the number of quarters creditable in a calendar year, then identify those quarters in which the non-citizen (or the parent(s) or spouse of the non-citizen) received Federal means-tested public benefits and then remove those quarters from the number of quarters of coverage earned or credited to the non-citizen in that calendar year. However, if the non-citizen earns the 40th quarter of coverage prior to applying for SNAP benefits or any other Federal means-tested public benefit in that same quarter, the State agency must allow that quarter toward the 40 qualifying quarters total; 2. A non-citizen admitted as a refugee under section 207 of the INA; 3. A non-citizen granted asylum under section 208 of the INA; 4. A non-citizen whose deportation is withheld under section 243(h) of the INA as in effect prior to April 1, 1997, or whose removal is withheld under section 241(b)(3) or the INA; 5. A non-citizen granted status as a Cuban or Haitian entrant (as defined in section 501(e) of the Refugee Education Assistance Act of 1980); 6. An Amerasian admitted pursuant to section 584 of Public Law 100- 202, as amended by Public Law 100-461; 7. A non-citizen with one of the following military connections: i. A veteran who was honorably discharged for reasons other than non-citizen status, who fulfills the minimum active-duty service requirements of 38 U.S.C. 5303A(d), including an individual who died in active military, naval or air service. The definition of veteran includes an individual who served before July 1, 1946, in the organized military forces of the Government of the Commonwealth of the Philippines while such forces were in the service of the Armed Forces of the U.S. or in the Philippine Scouts, as described in 38 U.S.C. 107; ii. An individual on active duty in the Armed Forces of the U.S. (other than for training); or iii. The spouse and unmarried dependent children of a person described in CFR 273.4 paragraphs (a)(6)(ii)(G)(1) or (a)(6)(ii)(G)(2) of this section, including the spouse of a deceased veteran, provided the marriage fulfilled the requirements of 38 U.S.C. 1304, and the spouse has not remarried. An unmarried dependent child for purposes of this CFR 273.4 paragraph (a)(6)(ii)(G)(3) is: a child who is under the age of 18 or, if a full-time student, under the age of 22; such unmarried dependent child of a deceased veteran provided such child was dependent upon the veteran at the time of the veteran's death; or an unmarried disabled child age 18 or older if the child was disabled and dependent on the veteran prior to the child's 18th birthday. For purposes of this CFR 273.4 paragraph (a)(6)(ii)(G)(3), child means the legally adopted or biological child of the person described in CFR 273.4 paragraph (a)(6)(ii)(G)(1) or (a)(6)(ii)(G)(2) of this section. NOTE: A person who is discharged or released from a period of active duty before completing the shorter of (A) 24 months of continuous active duty, or (B) the full period for which such person was called or ordered to active duty, is not eligible by reason of such period of active duty for any benefit under Federal Law (other than this title or any other law administered by the Secretary), and no dependent or survivor of such person shall be eligible for any such benefit by reason of such period of active duty of such person. Active duty in the Armed Forces of the United States means being on full-time duty in the United States Army, Navy, Air Force, Marine Corps, or Coast Guard. Duty for training is temporary full-time duty in the Armed Forces performed by members of the Reserves, Army National Guard, or Air National Guard for training purposes. Active duty for training does not establish eligible non-citizen status. NOTE: This provision applies to both non-citizens and citizens who are veterans or on active duty in the Armed Forces of the United States and to their alien spouses and non-citizens unmarried dependent children. A spouse is someone currently married under the laws of this State or whose marriage is recognized by this State as a legal marriage. This includes spouses who are residing apart regardless of whether a separation agreement is in effect. An unmarried dependent child of a veteran or active-duty member of the Armed Forces is a child who is: • The child (biological or adopted) or the stepchild of a veteran or active-duty member of the Armed Forces; and • Not married; and • Dependent on the veteran or active-duty member (can be claimed as a deduction on his or her federal income tax return). NOTE: The eligibility of an unmarried dependent child is not affected by the marital status of the parents. The DD-214 Certificate of Release or Discharge from Active Duty is the form provided to veterans by the Defense Department which documents the type of military discharge a veteran has been granted. Examples: If the spouse of a veteran is a non-citizen, but the veteran is a citizen, the spouse would meet the non- citizen eligibility criteria for SNAP. If a non-citizen veteran is divorced from his or her spouse, the veteran's unmarried dependent child would meet the non-citizen eligibility criteria for SNAP, regardless of which parent the child lives with. 8. An individual who is receiving benefits or assistance for blindness or disability (as specified in § 271.2 of this chapter). 9. An individual who on August 22, 1996, was lawfully residing in the U.S., and was born on or before August 22, 1931; or 10. A non-citizen who has been granted conditional entry pursuant to section 203(a)(7) of the Immigration and Naturalization Act as in effect prior to April 1, 1980. 11. An individual who is under 18 years of age. c. The following qualified non-citizens must be in qualified status for 5 years before being eligible to receive SNAP benefits. The 5 years in qualified status may be either consecutive or nonconsecutive. Temporary absences of less than 6 months from the United States with no intention of abandoning U.S. residency do not terminate or interrupt the individual’s period of U.S. residency. If the absence is longer than 6 months, it shall be presumed that U.S. residency was interrupted unless the non-citizen presents evidence of his or her intent to resume U.S. residency. 1. A non-citizen age 18 or older lawfully admitted for permanent residence under the Immigration and Naturalization Act; or 2. A non-citizen who is paroled into the U.S. under section 212(d)(5) of the Immigration and Naturalization Act for a period of a least 1 year; 3. A non-citizen who has been battered or subjected to extreme cruelty in the U.S. by a spouse or a parent or by a member of the spouse or parent’s family residing in the same household as the non-citizen at the time of the abuse, a non-citizen whose child has been battered or subjected to battery or cruelty, or an non-citizen child whose parent has been battered; d. Systems Support To aid in tracking non-citizen eligibility WMS edits have been changed to allow entries in the following fields for Case Types 31 (NPA-SNAP), 32 (SNAP-MIX) and 60 (HEAP): Citizenship Code, Alien Number, Date of Entry into United States, Nationality Code and Resettlement Agency Code. 2. Non-Citizens Lawfully Admitted for Permanent Residence with Qualifying Quarters of Coverage a. Definition and Basic Policy A permanent resident non-citizen is eligible to participate in the SNAP as a Specially Qualified Non-Citizen if: Is a lawful permanent resident (LPR) under the Immigration and Nationality Act. Such non-citizens are considered permanent residents and possess what is commonly called a "green card" (I-551); and Has earned 40 qualifying quarters of coverage as defined under Title II of the Social Security Act or can be credited with such qualifying quarters. b. Qualifying Quarters In determining the number of qualifying quarters of coverage set forth in (2) above, a non-citizen is credited with the following: i. All qualifying quarters of coverage worked by the non-citizen; ii. All qualifying quarters of coverage worked by a parent of such non- citizen before the non-citizen reached age 18; and All qualifying quarters worked by the spouse of such non-citizen during his/her marriage and the non-citizen remains married to such spouse or such spouse is deceased. Thus, a non-citizen may earn a qualifying quarter by either working or by being credited with a qualifying quarter worked by another. The same quarter can be credited to the person who worked as well as other family members eligible to be credited with such quarter. c. Who Can Be Credited With Work Quarters From Family Members A non-citizen can be credited with qualifying quarters earned by other family members. The following guidelines must be used to determine which family members can contribute qualifying quarters to the non-citizen. (1) A non-citizen who is married can be credited with work quarters worked, and any quarters worked by a spouse during the marriage. This applies so long as the non-citizen remains married to or separated from the spouse, or, if the spouse is deceased, was married to the spouse at the time of the spouse's death. The non-citizen loses all of their spouse's work quarters when divorced. The loss of the spouse's quarters must be used to determine SNAP eligibility when eligibility is re-determined during the certification period. (2) A non-citizen under age 18 can be credited with work quarters worked by both parents (including quarters earned prior to the child's birth), whether the parent is a non-citizen or not, and any of the non-citizen’s own quarters worked. (3) A non-citizen over age 18 can be credited with work quarters worked by both parents (including quarters earned prior to the child's birth) before that non-citizen reached age 18, whether the parent is a non-citizen or not, and any of the non-citizen's own quarters worked. d. Examples Of Crediting Quarters (1) If a non-citizen has earned 20 qualifying quarters, and his spouse has earned 20 qualifying quarters over the same time span, that non-citizen can be credited with 40 qualifying quarters in as few as 5 years. If the spouse is also a non-citizen, the spouse can also be credited with 40 qualifying quarters. If an LPR non-citizen is divorced from her husband, she cannot be credited with any of the qualifying quarters her former husband may have earned while they were married or since the divorce. If she qualified for SNAP benefits by being credited with qualifying quarters from her former spouse, she must have her eligibility based on non-citizen status re-determined at the first recertification subsequent to the divorce. If the LPR is separated, she would be credited with all of the quarters her spouse earned during the marriage and since the separation. e. Qualifying Quarters and Federal Means-Tested Public Benefit PRWORA established a special limitation on quarters worked after December 31, 1996. Quarters worked after December 31, 1996, do not count as qualifying quarters if the non-citizen receives any Federal means-tested public benefits during that quarter. (1) Federal means-tested programs include only those programs which: (a) Include federal dollars, and (b) Establish eligibility for program benefits based on income or income and resources. (2) This definition limits this prohibition to aliens who received assistance from one of the following programs during the quarter in question: (a) Federally participating public assistance (formerly ADC and EAF and now TANF and EAF), (b) Federal SNAP benefits, (c) Federally participating medical assistance, and (d) SSI benefits. (3) Specifically excluded from the definition of Federal means-tested public benefits are: Medicaid for certain emergency care, short-term non-cash emergency disaster relief, school lunch assistance, WIC assistance, public health services for treatment of communicable diseases and immunizations, foster care and adoption assistance under IV-B and E of the Social Security Act if the foster/adoptive parent is a potentially eligible non- citizen, certain programs providing assistance to students for educational purposes, Head Start benefits, JTPA benefits, and assistance which the United States Attorney General will designate. After December 31, 1996, a quarter in which the non-citizen received any Federal means-tested public benefit, as defined by the agency providing the benefit, or actually received SNAP benefits is not creditable toward the 40-quarter total. Likewise, a parent's or spouse's quarter is not creditable if the parent or spouse received any Federal means-tested public benefit or actually received SNAP benefits in that quarter. The State agency must evaluate quarters of coverage and receipt of Federal means-tested public benefits on a calendar year basis. The State agency must first determine the number of quarters creditable in a calendar year, then identify those quarters in which the non-citizen (or the parent(s) or spouse of the non- citizen) received Federal means-tested public benefits and then remove those quarters from the number of quarters of coverage earned or credited to the non- citizen in that calendar year. However, if the non-citizen earns the 40th quarter of coverage prior to applying for SNAP benefits or any other Federal means-tested public benefit in that same quarter, the State agency must allow that quarter toward the 40 qualifying quarters total. f. Income of Non-Citizens Ineligible for SNAP Due to Non-Citizen Status Current policy describes when the income of a non-citizen who is ineligible to participate in SNAP due to non-citizen status is budgeted to determine the household's SNAP benefits. See this section of the SNAPSB for instructions for determining household composition and Section 13 for instructions for budgeting the income of ineligible non-citizens. See SNAPSB Section 11 for instructions for budgeting deductions for households with ineligible non-citizens. (1) If the non-citizen is an ineligible member of the SNAP household, that person's income must be prorated and a prorated share excluded as meeting the non-citizen's needs and the balance budgeted as available to the SNAP household. This applies to all non-citizens who are mandated household members (children age 18 to 22 with income and not in school and residing with their parents, parents of minor children and spouses) and non-citizens who purchase and prepare food in common with the rest of the household. (2) If the non-citizen is not a mandated household member of the SNAP household, none of that non-citizen's income is budgeted. (3) The PRWORA made it possible for a SNAP household member who is a non- citizen to be eligible for TA but ineligible for SNAP. In such situations, a pro- rata share of the household's TA income is excluded from SNAP income. The balance of the household's TA income is budgeted for SNAP purposes. (4) In such situations, the household's SNAP benefits are calculated as follows: a. Divide the countable TA income evenly among the household members including the ineligible non-citizen. b. Subtract the ineligible non-citizen’s share from the total TA income and use the remainder as the TA income input on the SNAP budget. c. Reduce the number of SNAP household members by the number of ineligible(s) living with the household. Example 1: Suppose a single mother who is a permanent resident has 2 children, both of whom are U.S. citizens. She applies for TA and SNAP for herself and her children. She entered the U S as a permanent resident in March of 2011 and cannot be credited with 40 qualifying quarters. The mother and children are eligible for TA and receive a TA grant of $450 a month. However, only the children are eligible for SNAP benefits. To determine SNAP entitlement, the household’s TA grant is prorated. One-third ($150) is excluded as meeting the mother's needs and two-thirds ($300) is budgeted in determining the SNAP benefits for the 2 SNAP eligible children. Example 2: Suppose the mother's boyfriend, who is also an ineligible non- citizen, moves in with the family. He is not the father of either child. He states that he does not purchase and prepare in common with the mother and her children and the district determines he is not part of the mother's SNAP household. The boyfriend's income is not considered in determining the children's SNAP entitlement. Example 3: Suppose the boyfriend in example #2 is the father of one of the children or states that he purchases and prepares food in common with the mother and the children. His income is then prorated, and a prorated share is budgeted as available to the children. If his countable income is $400 a month, the district would divide his income by 4, the number of individuals in the household. His prorated share is $100, the mother's share is $100. The balance, $200, is budgeted as available to the children. g. Systems Support For Budgeting TA Only Cases On Upstate ABEL, workers must remember, after using Function Key 8 from a TA budget, to: (1) Reduce the number in the SNAP household by 1 (or however many ineligible non-citizens there are in the household) and (2) Reduce the countable TA grant brought over to the SNAP Input screen by ABEL, by the non-citizen’s pro-rata share of the countable TA grant. h. Systems Implications CNS language has been modified to reflect the alien provisions of PRWORA for the following SNAP individual reason codes: (1) F92, Ineligible Non-Citizen, and (2) F85 and F86, Failure to Verify Non-Citizen Status i. Documentation At the time of application or recertification for SNAP all individuals are required to certify in writing, under the penalty of perjury, that they are a United States Citizen, Native American, U.S. non-citizen National of the United States or a non- citizen with a satisfactory immigration status. Non-Citizen applicants must provide a USCIS document as evidence of a satisfactory immigration status for benefit eligibility. The amendments made by Section 121 of the Immigration Reform and Control Act (IRCA) of 1996 require a further step to verify the validity of the immigration document presented by the applicant. The Systematic Alien Verification for Entitlements (SAVE) provides a re-verification of the non-citizen’s documented immigration status against the United States Citizenship and Immigration Service’s (USCIS) Verification Information System (VIS) files to confirm the accuracy of the non-citizen documented immigration status established during application processing. SAVE is not intended to verify the applicant’s statement regarding his/her immigration status in lieu of documentation, nor does it shift the responsibility for establishing satisfactory immigration status from the applicant to the district. NOTE: For information regarding expired or lost immigration documents see 03 INF-19 Expired or Lost Immigration Documents j. Declaration of Citizenship/Immigration Status 1. One adult representative of a SNAP household must, as a condition of eligibility, attest to the citizenship/non-citizen status for all SNAP applicant/recipients. a. For applicants this declaration is found on page five, section ten of the LDSS-2921 - Application for Certain Benefits and Services . b. For recipients this declaration is found on page five, section nine of the LDSS-3174 - Recertification Form for Certain Benefits and Services. c. For NTA-SNAP applicants and recipients this declaration is found on page seven, section nine of the LDSS-4826 – Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) Application/Recertification 2. Exemptions - The following are the only situations which do not require completion of a declaration. a. An applicant/recipient (A/R) of MA or SNAP whose eligibility is established as a result of a separate determination process, and a completed declaration is already on file at the district; b. An SSI A/R who has applied for SNAP at a Social Security Administration (SSA) Office using the national application for SNAP [form FNS-385 (7-87)] since this application already includes the necessary declaration. 3. Failure or Refusal to Sign - Since the declaration of citizenship/immigrant status is accomplished when the last page of the application is signed; failure to sign is synonymous with failure to sign an application for SNAP. The only exception to this is if the applicant had signed page one of the application establishing a filing date and then does not sign the last page of the application. In such cases the application is denied for failure to cooperate. For TA/SNAP and mixed cases, individuals sanctioned from TA for failure to sign the declaration must be included in the SNAP household if otherwise eligible, if the general declaration on the last page of the application has been signed by any adult household member, or by an authorized representative. For examples of documentation which a non-citizen may present see the following resources: • LDSS-4579 Non-Citizen Eligibility Desk Aid • http://www.uscis.gov/i-9-central/acceptable-documents/list- documents k. Lack of Documentation Application For Verification - If the USCIS form I-94 does not contain the proper documentation and the non-citizen has no other verification of non-citizen classification in his or her possession, the district shall advise the non-citizen to submit form G-641, Application for Verification of Information from Immigration and Naturalization Service Records, to USCIS. Districts shall accept this form when presented by the alien and properly annotated at the bottom by an USCIS representative as evidence of lawful admission for permanent residence or parole for humanitarian reasons. Contacting USCIS- The non-citizen shall also be advised that: 1. Classification under Section 207, 208, 212(d)(5), or 243(h) of the Immigration and Nationality Act shall result in eligible status; 2. He/she may be eligible if acceptable verification is obtained; and 3. He/she may contact USCIS, as previously stated, or otherwise obtain the necessary verifications, or if the non-citizen wishes and signs a written consent that the district will contact USCIS to obtain clarification of the non- citizen's status. 4. If he/she does not wish to contact USCIS, the household may withdraw its application or participate without that member. Obtaining Other Acceptable Verification - If an non-citizen is unable to provide any USCIS document (not even an INS form I-94), then the district has no responsibility to offer to contact INS on the non-citizen's behalf. The district's responsibility exists only when the non-citizen has an INS document that does not clearly indicate eligible or ineligible status. In any event, the districts shall not contact INS to obtain information about the non-citizen's correct status without the non-citizen's written consent. If the proper USCIS documentation is not available, the non-citizen may state the reason and submit other conclusive verification. The district shall accept other forms of documentation or corroboration from USCIS that the non-citizen is classified pursuant to Section 101(a)(15), 101(a)(20), 207, 208, 212(d)(5), 243, or 249 of the INA, or other conclusive evidence such as a court order stating that deportation has been withheld pursuant to Section 243(h) of the INA. Note: see the LDSS-4579 Non-Citizen Eligibility Desk Aid for a description of the types of documentation that may be presented for confirmation of status. l. Status While Awaiting Verification A non-citizen is ineligible until acceptable documentation is provided unless: 1. The State agency has submitted a copy of a document provided by the household to USCIS for verification. Pending such verification, the State agency cannot delay, deny, reduce or terminate the individual's eligibility for benefits on the basis of the individual's immigration status; or 2. The applicant or the State agency has submitted a request to SSA for information regarding the number of quarters of work that can be credited to the individual, SSA has responded that the individual has fewer than 40 quarters, and the individual provides documentation from SSA that SSA is conducting an investigation to determine if more quarters can be credited. If SSA indicates that the number of qualifying quarters that can be credited is under investigation, the State agency must certify the individual pending the results of the investigation for up to 6 months from the date of the original determination of insufficient quarters; or 3. The applicant or the local district has submitted a request to a Federal agency for verification of information which bears on the individual's eligible non-citizen status. The local district must certify the individual pending the results of the investigation for up to 6 months from the date of the original request for verification. The local district must provide alien applicants with a reasonable opportunity to submit acceptable documentation of their eligible non-citizen status as of the 30th day following the date of application. A reasonable opportunity must be at least 10 days from the date of the local districts request for an acceptable document. When the local district fails to provide a non-citizen applicant with a reasonable opportunity as of the 30th day following the date of application, the local district must provide the household with benefits no later than 30 days following the date of application, provided the household is otherwise eligible. m. Other Ineligible Non-Citizens Unless listed above, non-citizens are ineligible to participate in the program as a member of any household. Among those ineligible to participate are non-citizens who are: • Visitors; • Tourists; • Diplomats; and • Students on a temporary visa with no intention of abandoning their residence in a foreign country. n. Illegal Non-Citizens The Welfare Reform Act of 1997 established that each district must report to OTDA the name and address and other identifying information known to it with respect to any non-citizen known to be unlawfully in the United States. OTDA has identified two specific conditions which definitively establish that a non-citizen is unlawfully present. Accordingly, the report of "Aliens Unlawfully in the United States: Attachment IX to 97 ADM-23 - Public Assistance (Family Assistance/Safety Net Assistance) Changes Resulting From The Welfare Reform Act of 1997, should only include non-citizens who meet either of the following two conditions: 1. Non-Citizens with a final INS Order of Deportation outstanding. An outstanding order of deportation is final when it is not subject to appeal, either because: a. The relevant statutory appeal period (10 days) has expired, b. There are no lawful grounds upon which an appeal may be based, or, c. The available administrative and/or judicial appeals have been exhausted and the order is not subject to review under the limited standards for reopening or reconsideration. 2. Non-Citizens for which the SAVE response to an electronic or a manually submitted INS G-845, (Document Verification Request) indicates that the person has submitted false immigration documents to the district. When either of the above situations is applicable to an applicant or recipient of temporary assistance (FA or SNA), you should submit the name(s) and address(es) of the individual(s), along with identification of the documentation establishing their being unlawfully present in the U.S., including copies of the documentation, when possible, to: NYS Office of Temporary and Disability Assistance Division of Temporary Assistance - 9th Floor 40 North Pearl Street, Albany, N Y 12243 This information should be provided on a copy of the revised reporting form, "Aliens Unlawfully in the United States” Attachment IX to 97 ADM- 23. You should submit the form within 10 days from the end of any month in which you identify "reportable" non-citizens. NOTE: There are no reporting requirements for MA or SNAP. When an applicant is checked as being only for MA and/or SNAP, you should not include them on the report. 3. Districts should refer any non-citizen requesting TA benefits who fails to furnish evidence that he or she is lawfully residing in the United States to the INS office, or the nearest consulate of the country of the applicant or recipient. Referral means informing the non-citizen of the location of the nearest INS office or the nearest consulate and advising the non-citizen that proper documentation should be secured. The proper documentation is required if you are to proceed with the processing of their TA application to determine what, if any, benefits the non-citizen would be eligible to receive. Students 1. Definitions a. Student - A student is any individual who is enrolled at least half-time in an institution of higher education (including on-line courses and correspondence schools), excluding high school and on the job training programs. b. Institution Of Higher Education - Any institution which normally requires a high school diploma or equivalency certificate for enrollment including, but not limited to, colleges, universities, and vocational or technical schools at the post high school level. (The Institution of Higher Education determines whether or not a high school diploma or equivalency certificate is necessary for enrollment.) 2. Student Eligibility Exemption Criteria In order to participate in the SNAP a student must meet one of the following exemption criteria: a. Are under age 18 or age 50 or older b. Have a physical or mental disability c. Work an average of at least 20 hours a week in paid employment The district should determine the weekly average using the hours worked during the 4 weeks prior to the date of application or re-application (recertification). Example: • Prior week 1 – 16 hours worked • Prior Week 2 – 21 hours worked • Prior Week 3 – 25 hours worked • Prior Week 4 – 24 hours worked 86 total hours worked in past 4 week/4 = 21.5 average hours per week. If the number of hours worked during the prior 4 week period does not provide an accurate indication of the number of hours to be worked weekly, district may use a longer period than just the past 4 weeks, such as the past 8 weeks, to gain a more accurate indication of the weekly hours being worked. If the hours that will be worked during the new certification period will be different from the hours worked during the prior 4 weeks, then the anticipated number of hours to be worked should be used to determine the average hours worked. If the exact amount of the anticipated hours to be worked is not known, that portion that can be determined with reasonable certainty should be used. d. Be self-employed for at least an average of at least 20 hours per week, and receive weekly earnings at least equal to the hourly Federal minimum wage multiplied by 20 hours SCENARIO 1: James Smart is a full-time student applying for food stamp benefits. He is employed by a local supermarket for an average of 12 hours per week stocking shelves. Because these are paid employed hours, these hours count toward his total weekly average. He also works on average, 10 hours per week building websites for customers. He has been making, on average, $50 per week on his website work. This averages out to $5/hour. This is below the current federal minimum wage of $7.25/hour. Therefore, these hours do not count toward James’s weekly employment hours. James has an average of 12 hours of eligible paid employment per week, and therefore does not meet this criteria for student eligibility. SCENARIO 2: James Smart is a full-time student applying for food stamp benefits. He is employed by a local supermarket for an average of 12 hours per week stocking shelves. Because these are paid employed hours, these hours count toward his total weekly average. James also earns an average of $100/week for 10 hours of self-employed work building websites, which equals an average hourly wage of $10, which is above the federal minimum wage of $7.25. Therefore these hours would count toward James’s weekly employment hours. James has an average of 22 hours of eligible paid employment per week (12 hours at the supermarket and 10 hours building websites), and therefore, he meets the criteria for student eligibility. e. Participate in a State or federally financed work study program during the regular school year as follows; (1) The work study must be approved at the time of application for SNAP or at the time that the individual becomes a student as defined by SNAP rules, and (2) The work study must be approved for the school term; and (3) The student must actually anticipate working during that time. f. Participate in an on-the-job training program g. Care for a child under the age of 6 h. Care for a child age 6 to 11 and lack the necessary child care enabling you to attend school and work 20 hours or participate in a work study i. Are a single parent enrolled full-time in college and taking care of a child under 12 j. Receive Temporary Assistance for Needy Families k. Be enrolled as a result of participation in the Job Opportunities and Basic Skills program under Title IV of the Social Security Act or its successor program l. Are assigned to, placed in, or self-placed in a college or other institution of higher education through: (1) A SNAP Employment and Training (SNAPE&T) program (2) Certain other E&T programs for low-income households, which are operated by a state or local government and have an equivalent component to SNAP E&T (3) A program under Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act (WIOA) (4) A Trade Adjustment Assistance Program under Section 236 of the Trade Act of 1974 m. Attend a SUNY or CUNY comprehensive college, technology college, or community college and are enrolled in a qualified career and technical education program. n. Attend any of the 10 Educational Opportunity Centers (EOC) in New York State and are enrolled in a career and technical education program, remedial course, basic adult education, literacy, or English as a second language The qualifying exemption begins in the month that the school term begins or the month that the work study is approved, whichever is later. The qualifying exemption continues until the end of the school term or until the district becomes aware that the student has refused an assignment. The exemption does not continue between terms when there is a break of a full month or longer unless the student is participating in work study during the break. In order to be eligible for SNAP during a school break, an individual who is defined as a student by SNAP rules and is otherwise eligible must meet one of the other exemptions outlined in this section. NOTE: An applicant or recipient age 18 through 49, physically and mentally fit, enrolled at least half-time in an institution of higher education and in receipt of UIB is considered to be an eligible student for SNAP purposes, if the Department of Labor (DOL) has approved their program. If approved, the student will have a received a TCC 316.1 document and can request copies of this document by calling DOL, Special Programs Unit at 518-402-0189. NOTE: A student who participates in a meal plan providing less than the majority of the student's monthly meals is not considered a resident of an institution. If the student also meets the eligible student criteria and is otherwise eligible, the student is allowed to receive SNAP benefits. In situations where the number of meals to be consumed by the student is unclear due to the optional nature of the meal plan, the applicant must state the number of meals that are anticipated to be consumed on and off the meal plan. If the majority of meals stated are off the plan for the month, the student may be considered for participation on SNAP. In these cases, the student /SNAP recipient should be reminded of the obligation to report changes to the district, particularly with regard to changes in the number of meals to be consumed on the plan. 3. Ineligible Student a. For treatment of income of an ineligible student see SNAPSB Section 13. b. For treatment of resources of an ineligible student see SNAPSB Section 17. 4. School Enrollment The enrollment status of a student shall begin on the first day of the school term of the institution of higher education. Such enrollment shall be deemed to continue through normal periods of class attendance, vacation, and recess, unless the student graduates, is suspended or expelled, drops out or does not intend to register for the next school term (excluding summer school). Employment Registration For implications regarding initial eligibility see the SNAP Employment Policy manual. Reference: 387.9 Investigation and Eligibility SNAP Employment Policy Manual N. Social Security Numbers 1. Requirement For Participation a. All households applying for or participating in the SNAP shall provide the Social Security Number (SSN) of each household member or apply for one before certification. Disqualification of the individual from participation in the SNAP is a consequence of a refusal to apply for or to provide the SSN. Applying for or providing the SSN to the district immediately brings the disqualified individual into compliance with this requirement. NOTE: If individuals have more than one number, all numbers shall be required. b. SNAP household members who refuse to provide or apply for an SSN because of a valid religious belief are not to be disqualified from SNAP participation. They are to be included as SNAP household members if otherwise eligible. Documentation must clearly be recorded in the case file if household members indicate they refuse to provide or apply for a SSN because of a valid religious belief. This documentation may include obtaining written or verbal confirmation from the religious organization the individual is a member of that providing of an SSN by its members is in conflict with its religious doctrine. a. TA regulations do not allow a waiver from the requirement to provide or apply for an SSN based on religious beliefs. Such TA households, if otherwise SNAP eligible, would be processed for SNAP as a mixed household including the non-TA SSN religious objector as a SNAP household member. b. Social Security Number Code "4-SSN Not Applied For" is to be entered on the WMS case record for household members that refuse to provide or apply for an SSN because of religious belief. 2. Obtaining SSN's For SNAP Household Members a. For those individuals who provide SSNs prior to certification, recertification or at any office contact, the district shall record the number and verify it in accordance with this section. b. Those individuals who do not have an SSN, are unsure if they have one, or are unable to find it should be referred to a Social Security Administration (SSA) Field Office (FO). The district shall inform the household where to apply and what information will be needed. The district shall require proof of application. a. Effective August 1, 2014, SSA FOs no longer provide print outs of SSN. If an applicant or recipient needs to provide documentation that they have applied for a new replacement, or corrected SSN they can apply through the SSA account portal at www.socialsecurity.gov/myaccount, the national toll-free number 1(800)772-1213 or at a SSA FO. b. If the individual applies at a Field Office, the SSA representative will provide the applicant/recipient with a SSA-5028 “Receipt for Application for a Social Security Number”. This receipt is documentation that the individual has complied with applying for a new, replacement, or corrected SSN. c. If an individual wishes to apply at the SSA office, d. Any person who is foreign born and applying for either a new Social Security card or a replacement must be referred to SSA for enumeration. NOTE: Districts should not take an SS-5 from, or refer to SSA, an undocumented immigrant who will be denied benefits or whose benefits will be terminated. e. Any person who is 18 years of age or older and applying for an original SSN must be referred to SSA for enumeration. Parents/caretakers must apply at the local SSA field office for an original SSN for children under 18. f. Enumeration At Birth Program Many hospitals are participating in this program whereby the hospital files an SS-5 (Application for an SSN) for the newborn. A revised federal form SSA-2853, "Information About When You Will Receive Your Baby's Social Security Card", when signed and dated by a hospital official, is acceptable verification that the household has applied for an SSN for the newborn, if the district has other information that connects the child named to a member of the household, e.g., the name of the mother which may be obtained from the hospital, birth document, a birth certificate, or other reliable information. NOTE: WMS report - WINR-5129 - Newborns With No Social Security Number on WMS - has been developed to serve as a reminder to workers to pursue a Social Security Number for each newborn listed. The report will list any individual born in the previous month with no Social Security Number on WMS. The report will include a district page plus a Unit/Worker page. In order to properly utilize this report, as well as assure the most complete and accurate data, workers must be prompt in adding newborns to the SNAP household and entering them on WMS. This report along with the WINR-5126-Individuals With Incorrect or no Social Security Number on WMS - will ensure that SSN's have been obtained and verified for all SNAP household members. 3. Failure To Comply If the district determines that a household member(s) has refused to provide or apply for an SSN, then the individual without the SSN shall be ineligible to participate in the SNAP. The disqualification applies to the individual(s) for whom the SSN is not provided and not to the entire household. The disqualified household member(s) may become eligible upon providing the district with an SSN or proof of application for an SSN. The income and resources of an individual disqualified from the household for failure to comply with this requirement shall be handled as set forth in SNAPSB Section 13 and Section 17. 4. Use Of SSNs The district is authorized to use SSNs in the administration of the SNAP. Social Security numbers shall be used to: a. Verify information regarding SNAP applicants and participants who receive SSI benefits. The State Data Exchange (SDX) shall be used to the greatest extent possible. b. Prevent duplicate participation. c. Facilitate mass changes in Federal benefits. d. Determine the accuracy and/or reliability of information given by households. References: 387.9(a) Investigation and Eligibility 273.2 (f) (1) (v) Office operations and application processing 273.6 Social Security Numbers 90 ADM-23 Changes in Social Security Number Policy 99 INF-7 Amended State Version of Federal Form SS-5 "Application For A Social Security Card" GIS Message (14 TA/DC027) Changes to the Services Provided by Social Security Offices O. Validation of Social Security Number 1. WMS SSN Validation Process a. The WMS SSN Validation process is used to validate SSNs through SSA. All SSNs entered on WMS/MyWorkspace with a SSN Code 1 (SSN present) will be sent by OTDA to SSA to match Social Security number information on a regular basis. b. If an applicant or a household member does not have a Social Security number and has applied for one, the SSN Code 2 (SSN Applied For) is entered into WMS/MyWorkspace. When the individual provides their SSN, the worker must change the SSN Code 2 to SSN Code 1 to start the validation process. c. When the SSN match results are received from SSA, the SSN Code field on WMS will be automatically updated. Additionally, to assist districts in ensuring that SSNs are present and accurate in WMS, system-generated management reports are available through the Benefit Issuance Control System (BICS). d. If the individuals SSN passes the validation process, the SSN Code of 1 will be automatically updated to a SSN Code of 8 (SSA Validated SSN). If the individual’s SSN fails the validation process, the SSN Code of 1 will be automatically updated to a SSN Code indicating the SSN failed the validation process and the worker must investigate why the validation failed. If the worker finds that information was incorrectly entered into WMS/MyWorkspace, they should correct the information. Otherwise, the applicant must contact their local SSA office to apply for a correct SSN. 2. Actions To Be Taken As A Result Of Matches The results of matches between OTDA and SSA will be transmitted by OTDA to districts in two ways: a. SSN Is Validated - If the SSN is validated via the match, WMS/MyWorkspace will automatically be updated and a new "Code 8 - SSA SSN Validation" will appear in the SSN field. No further action is needed on these cases. b. SSN Fails Validation - If the SSN fails validation: (1) "Code 9 - SSN Failed SSA Validation" will appear on the SSN field on WMS on appropriate inquiry screens and MyWorkspace summary and individual tabs. (2) OTDA will transmit a new report, WINR 9311, "The Social Security Number Validation Report" to districts which will contain a list of cases that failed validation and the reasons for the discrepancies. (3) Separate reports will be sent to each program area, TA, MA, SNAP and Services. It should be noted that only cases highest in the WMS case type hierarchy will be reported on the exception report when there is a multi-case involvement. The hierarchy is: TA, MA, SNAP and Services. For example, a TA/MA/SNAP case will be reported on the TA report, while an MA /SNAP case will be reported on the MA report. This will require coordination at the local level between program areas. 3. Resolving Error Messages a. Upon receipt of the WINR 9311, districts must examine the report and take appropriate action on each of the cases. The following error messages will be generated for SSN's which fail validation. These messages are stated specifically on the report in the "message" column. • SSN is not on SSA file • Name matches, date of birth (DOB) matches, sex code does not match • Name matches, sex code matches, DOB does not match • Name matches, DOB and sex codes do not match • Name does not match, DOB and sex codes not checked b. The district shall be responsible for resolving any discrepancy by checking the information received from the applicant/recipient with the information on the WMS data base. This may require contacting the individual for additional information. After correcting the discrepancy, the worker must change the SSN Validation code field in the WMS data base to Code 1 (SSN present) which will allow the SSN to be validated at the next update. c. This review must be completed no later than the next client contact. (Next client contact includes mail-in recertifications.) Districts should annotate the case record when there is a change made to the name, sex or DOB and the case is recorded as code 1. This will remind the worker what the original match data was in the event that the second validation match is returned with another discrepancy. This procedure will avoid re-entering "bad" demographic data. 4. Resolving Discrepancies When resolving discrepancies, the following actions must also be taken in the situations outlined below: a. Incorrect SSN - If the information on file at SSA is incorrect, a new SS-5 (Application for Social Security Number) must be completed as this is SSA's procedure for correcting information on their files. Additionally, if the error message indicates that the SSN is not on file at the SSA, a new SS-5 must be completed. A number that has been assigned by the enumeration process (Code 7) does not have to be validated again. b. Name Does Not Match - If the message/error indicates that the name does not match and further investigation indicates that the name on file with SSA is not the same name known by social services, the worker must inform the applicant/recipient that he/she must use the same name for both programs. The individual has the right to decide which name he/she wants to be known by. If he/she chooses to change his/her name with SSA, a new SS-5 must be completed indicating the new name. c. Multiple SSN's - Occasionally, an individual will submit more than one SSN and indicate that they all belong to him/her. Such individuals should be referred to the local SSA office. SSA will cross-reference the multiple numbers and advise the applicant which SSN should be used. However, SSA cannot cancel a number that has been assigned. 5. Refusal To Cooperate In Completion Of An SS-5 To Resolve An SSN Validation Discrepancy a. Refusal of a SNAP household to cooperate in completion of an SS-5 to resolve an SSN validation discrepancy shall result in the SNAP case being terminated. If one member of the household refuses to cooperate in the validation process, the district must determine if there is another household member who is able and is willing to cooperate in resolving the discrepancy before terminating the household. Such households subject to termination are to be issued a timely notice of adverse action informing them that their SNAP benefits are being terminated for refusing to cooperate in furnishing correct information to enable the district to verify a household member's SSN. b. A household that has been terminated for failing to cooperate in resolving an SSN validation discrepancy may reapply for SNAP benefits but will not be eligible until they cooperate in resolving the SSN discrepancy. 6. WMS Implications All active individuals on the WMS system with SSN Code "1 - SSN Present" will be selected for validation with SSA's automated validation/verification system. Two WMS SSN codes have been developed for this process. Code "8 - SSN SSA Validation" and Code "9 - SSN Failed SSA Validation". Both of these codes will be system-generated on WMS by the SSN Validation process. a. Code 8 - SSN SSA Validation - When the SSN entered on WMS/MyWorkspace matches SSA's record, and when the name, sex and date of birth similarly correspond to SSA's record, Code "8 - SSN SSA Validation" will be system- generated on the WMS/MyWorkspace case record, overlaying the current SSN code. Code 8 - SSN SSA Validation can be entered by the worker when it appears on the WMS Clearance Report and is used in conjunction with a CIN number at subsequent case openings and re-openings. WMS Code 8 (SSN SSA Validation) satisfies the federal QC requirement for social security number validation. Code 8 in the SSN-CD field on screen 2 is sufficient documentation for QC purposes. Although no error will be cited when a number is coded 8, whenever available the districts should keep a copy of the social security card in the permanent documentation file. At case openings and recertification eligibility workers should check to make sure the names and SSN's of applicants/recipients match those on WMS. This will ensure the accuracy of any automated matches. b. Code 9 - SSN Failed SSA Validation - When either the SSN fails to match SSA's record, or the name, sex or date of birth associated with the SSN fails to correspond to SSA's record, Code "9 -SSN Failed SSA Validation" will be system-generated on the WMS/MyWorkspace case record, overlaying the current SSN code. NOTE: WMS/MyWorkspace prohibits a change to an SSN for an individual currently having an SSN Code of "8 - SSN Validation" and will require a change from an SSN Code of "9 - SSN Failed SSA Validation" to Code "1 - SSN Present" whenever a demographic change is made to name, sex, date of birth, or SSN. 7. WINR 9311 "The Social Security Number Validation Report" This report is produced after each match with SSA and contains three parts: a. Part I - SSA Rejections - This part lists all the individuals whose SSN or demographics mismatch the SSA files. b. Part II - WMS Rejections - This part lists any individuals who were unavailable for update on WMS, e.g. SSN was changed while the original SSN was being sent for SSA validation. c. Part III - WMS Updates for SSN Validation - This part contains the total number of SSNs validated. Reference: 90 ADM-23 Changes in Social Security Number Policy 99 INF-7 Amended State Version of Federal Form SS-5 "Application For A Social Security Card" 15TA/DC035 Validation of Social Security Numbers 14 INF 05 Resource File Integration (RFI) Policy and Procedures P. Transfer of Resources Households which have transferred resources knowingly for the purpose of qualifying or attempting to qualify for SNAP benefits shall be disqualified from participation in the program. See SNAPSB Section 17 for specific provisions on the transfer of resources. Reference: 387.9(a) Investigation and Eligibility 273.8 (h) Resource Eligibility Standards Q. Household Cooperation 1. Household Cooperation A household applying for SNAP benefits must cooperate in the eligibility determination process. The eligibility determination process requires that the application form be completed and signed, the household or its authorized representative be interviewed, and certain information on the application be verified. To designate an authorized representative, the applicant must do so in writing. This can be done using the application or recertification application form or the LDSS – 4942 Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP)Authorized Representative Request Form, although any written designation is sufficient. a. If a household refuses to cooperate in completing the eligibility determination process, the application must be denied at the time of refusal. A household has refused to cooperate when the household is able to cooperate, but clearly demonstrates that it will not take actions that are required to complete the application process. The failure of a household to appear at a scheduled interview does not constitute a refusal to cooperate unless the household has clearly refused to be interviewed. If there is any question as to whether the household has merely failed to cooperate, as opposed to refused to cooperate, the household's application must not be denied. b. A household's eligibility must be terminated if it refuses to cooperate with any subsequent review of its eligibility, including reviews generated by reported changes in circumstances and applications for recertification. c. A household whose eligibility has been denied or terminated for refusal to cooperate may reapply for SNAP benefits. Such household must not be determined eligible until it cooperates in completing the eligibility determination process. (1) A household receiving SNAP benefits or reapplying for SNAP benefits must cooperate with any quality control review of its current or previous SNAP eligibility. A household refusing to cooperate in a quality control review must be determined ineligible to receive SNAP benefits. Quality control reviews are conducted by NYS OTDA Audit and Quality Improvement staff. d. A SNAP household cannot be denied for failure to cooperate with a FEDs interview. e. A household whose eligibility has been terminated for refusal to cooperate with a quality control review may reapply for SNAP. Such household must not be determined eligible until it cooperates in the quality control review. f. A household cannot be denied SNAP for its refusal to cooperate in a quality control review if: (1) The household was terminated for refusing to cooperate with a quality control review during the completed quality control review period; and (2) The household reapplied for SNAP more than 125 days after the end of the annual review period, if it was terminated for refusal to cooperate with a State quality control review, or (3) The household reapplies more than nine months after the end of the annual review period, if it was terminated for refusal to cooperate with a federal quality control review; and (4) The household provides verification of its eligibility for SNAP in accordance with this section. g. TA QC Reviews - Because a QC review of the TA case does not include a review of the same household's SNAP case, State regulation 387.9(a)(7)(ii) will not support the closing of the SNAP case when the federal sample TA case is closed for failure to comply with QC. h. Automated Finger Imaging System (AFIS) Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program: As of November 1, 2012, districts are prohibited from finger imaging any member of a household applying for or receiving SNAP benefits for any purpose related to SNAP. Effective November 1, 2012, all SNAP Automated Finger Imaging System (AFIS) Plans of Operation submitted by any and all districts are hereby null and void. NOTE: Temporary Assistance: Districts must continue to follow current TA policy regarding finger imaging. Members of households who are jointly applying for or jointly receiving TA benefits and SNAP benefits must continue to be finger imaged for TA purposes. However, if a person or case is being denied benefits for TA due to a failure or refusal to be finger imaged, a separate determination for SNAP eligibility must be done. Further, if an active TA case is being closed due to the failure or refusal of a TA case member to be finger imaged, a separate determination of SNAP eligibility must be done, including an assessment of eligibility for Transitional Benefits Alternative (TBA) SNAP benefits. If an individual is being removed from a TA case due to a failure or refusal to be finger imaged, a separate determination of SNAP eligibility must be done for the SNAP household.
